title: Install UE5 & Tour the Editor
html:
Getting Unreal onto your machine

Unreal Engine 5 ships through the Epic Games Launcher. You do not download the engine directly; you install the launcher first, then add engine versions from inside it.

  1. Go to unrealengine.com, create a free Epic account, and download the Epic Games Launcher for your OS.
  2. Open the launcher, sign in, and click the Unreal Engine tab on the left.
  3. Open the Library sub-tab. Next to Engine Versions, click the + and pick the latest 5.x release (e.g. 5.4).
  4. Click Install. Expect 30–60 GB. Under Options you can uncheck platforms you do not need (Android, iOS, Linux) to save space, but keep Starter Content and Engine Source if you plan to write C++.
C++ needs a compiler.
On Windows install Visual Studio 2022 (Community is free) with the Game development with C++ workload AND the Desktop development with C++ workload. On macOS install Xcode from the App Store and run it once to accept the license. Without these, C++ projects will not compile.

Creating your first project

Hit Launch on your engine version. The Project Browser opens. Pick a category (Games), then a template. For now choose Third Person. Set:

  • Blueprint vs C++ — choose Blueprint for this first project; you can add C++ later.
  • Quality Preset — Maximum for desktop.
  • Starter Content — leave on; it gives you meshes and materials.
  • Project name & location — avoid spaces and deep paths; e.g. D:/UE/FirstProto.

Touring the editor

The editor opens to the Level Editor. Learn these regions:

RegionWhat it is
Viewport (center)The 3D view of your level.
Outliner (top-right)A tree of every Actor in the level.
Details (bottom-right)Properties of whatever is selected.
Content Drawer (bottom)Your asset browser — meshes, materials, Blueprints.
Toolbar (top)Save, Play (PIE), platform/build settings.

Navigating the viewport

This is the single most important skill. With the viewport focused:

  • Fly mode: hold Right Mouse Button and use W A S D to fly; Q/E drop/raise; mouse wheel changes speed.
  • Orbit: hold Alt + Left Mouse to tumble around the selection.
  • Pan: hold Middle Mouse and drag.
  • Focus: select an Actor and press F to frame it.
Press Play (Alt+P).
This is Play In Editor (PIE). The Third Person template drops you in as a controllable character. Press Esc or click Stop to return to editing.
Save early, save the level.
Ctrl+S saves the current level. File > Save All saves every dirty asset. The engine does not autosave your edits to disk by default.
🎮 Build This: First Prototype Flythrough

Open a template level and fly the camera around a placed set of meshes.

  1. Create a Third Person project with Starter Content.
  2. Open the ThirdPersonMap (or the Open World template) from the Content Drawer.
  3. Drag two or three meshes from StarterContent/Props into the viewport.
  4. Practice fly-cam: RMB + WASD to orbit the scene, F to focus each mesh.
  5. Press Play, walk the character around your props, then Stop and Save All.

title: Projects, Levels & Placing Actors
html:
How a project is organized

A UE5 project is a folder containing a .uproject file, a Content/ folder (all your assets), and — if you use C++ — Source/, Config/, and generated Binaries/ and Intermediate/ folders. The Content Drawer mirrors Content/ on disk.

Folder discipline pays off.
Make top-level folders like Maps/, Blueprints/, Materials/, Meshes/, UI/. Never move or rename assets in your OS file explorer — always do it inside the Content Drawer so the engine can fix references.

Levels (maps)

A Level is a single .umap file holding a collection of Actors. Create one with File > New Level and pick Empty Level or Basic (Basic gives you a floor, light, and sky). Save it into Content/Maps/.

To make a level load when the game starts, set it in Edit > Project Settings > Maps & Modes > Default Maps. There are two slots: Editor Startup Map and Game Default Map.

Placing Actors

An Actor is anything you can place in a level. Add them via:

  • Drag a Static Mesh from the Content Drawer into the viewport.
  • Use the + Add button (or the Place Actors panel) for primitives: Cube, Sphere, Plane, lights, cameras, triggers.

Add a cube, then resize it into a floor. With it selected, the Details panel shows the Transform: Location, Rotation, Scale.

The transform gizmos

Switch tools with keyboard:

KeyGizmo
WMove (translate)
ERotate
RScale

Drag the colored axes to move along X (red), Y (green), Z (blue). Toggle grid snapping from the icons on the top-right of the viewport — snap moves to e.g. 10 units and rotations to 15 degrees so geometry lines up cleanly.

Type exact transforms.
For a blockout, click into the Details numbers and type values. A 1000×1000 floor: scale a Plane to X=10, Y=10 (the default Plane is 100 units). Walls: scale a Cube thin and tall and place along the edges.

Collision and walking through your space

Static Mesh primitives ship with simple collision, so the default character can walk on the floor and into walls. If you place a custom mesh and fall through it, open the mesh asset and check Collision > Add Box Simplified Collision, or set Collision Complexity to Use Complex as Simple.

No PlayerStart, no spawn.
An empty level has nowhere to place the player. From + Add, search Player Start and drop it slightly above the floor, or PIE will spawn the pawn at the world origin (possibly under the floor).
🎮 Build This: Blockout Room

A small playable space you can walk through with the default pawn.

  1. New Level (Basic). Save it as Maps/Blockout.
  2. Add a Plane scaled to 10×10 as the floor.
  3. Add four Cubes, scale them thin/tall, and place them as walls snapped to the floor edges (use grid snap).
  4. Drag a couple of Starter Content props inside as obstacles; give custom meshes simple collision if needed.
  5. Place a Player Start in the room. Press Play and walk the room. Save All.

title: Blueprints & C++: When to Use Each
html:
Two ways to write the same logic

Unreal gives you Blueprints (a visual node graph) and C++ (compiled code). They are not rivals — they interoperate. A common pro pattern: write the systems and performance-critical logic in C++, then expose tunable values and per-instance behavior to Blueprint for designers.

Reach for Blueprint when…Reach for C++ when…
Prototyping fast, iterating on feelBuilding reusable base classes / systems
Wiring up UI, level scripting, one-offsHeavy per-frame math, large loops
Designers need to tweak itYou want version-control-friendly diffs
Event-driven gameplay glueNetworking internals, save systems

A Blueprint pickup, described

Create a Blueprint: Content Drawer > + Add > Blueprint Class > Actor, name it BP_Pickup. Open it. In the Components panel, add a Static Mesh (set it to a shape) as the root visual.

Switch to the Event Graph. To spin it: drag off the Event Tick node, search AddLocalRotation, target the mesh component, and feed a Rotator. Multiply the yaw by Delta Seconds (from Tick) times a speed variable so it is framerate-independent.

To change color over time: create a Dynamic Material Instance on BeginPlay (node Create Dynamic Material Instance, assign back to the mesh), then on Tick call Set Vector Parameter Value with an HSV-cycled color into a parameter your material exposes.

Graph reading order.
White wires are execution (the order things run); colored wires are data. Execution flows left to right out of red event nodes like BeginPlay and Tick.

The same pickup in C++

Add a C++ class: Tools > New C++ Class > Actor, name it SpinPickup. Edit the source:

SpinPickup.h
UCLASS() class FIRSTPROTO_API ASpinPickup : public AActor { GENERATED_BODY() public: ASpinPickup(); virtual void Tick(float DeltaTime) override; UPROPERTY(VisibleAnywhere) UStaticMeshComponent* Mesh; UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere, Category="Spin") float SpinSpeed = 90.f; // degrees/sec private: UMaterialInstanceDynamic* DynMat = nullptr; float Hue = 0.f; };
SpinPickup.cpp
ASpinPickup::ASpinPickup() { PrimaryActorTick.bCanEverTick = true; Mesh = CreateDefaultSubobject<UStaticMeshComponent>(TEXT("Mesh")); RootComponent = Mesh; } void ASpinPickup::BeginPlay() { Super::BeginPlay(); DynMat = Mesh->CreateAndSetMaterialInstanceDynamic(0); } void ASpinPickup::Tick(float DeltaTime) { Super::Tick(DeltaTime); AddActorLocalRotation(FRotator(0.f, SpinSpeed * DeltaTime, 0.f)); Hue = FMath::Fmod(Hue + DeltaTime * 60.f, 360.f); if (DynMat) DynMat->SetVectorParameterValue("Color", FLinearColor::MakeFromHSV8((uint8)(Hue/360.f*255), 255, 255)); }
Macros are mandatory.
UCLASS(), UPROPERTY(), and GENERATED_BODY() are not decoration — they generate reflection code that lets the editor see your class. Forget EditAnywhere on a property and it will not show in the Details panel; forget UPROPERTY on a pointer and the garbage collector may delete your object.
Compile after every C++ change.
Use the editor's Live Coding (Ctrl+Alt+F11) for quick rebuilds, or build from your IDE. Blueprints compile when you click Compile in their editor.
🎮 Build This: Spinning Color Pickup (twice)

One spinning, color-changing pickup as a Blueprint, and again as a C++ Actor.

  1. Make a material with a Color vector parameter plugged into Base Color.
  2. Build BP_Pickup with Tick rotation + a dynamic material color cycle.
  3. Build ASpinPickup in C++ using the snippets above; assign the same material.
  4. Place both side by side, press Play, and confirm they behave identically.

title: The Gameplay Framework: GameMode, Pawn & PlayerController
html:
The cast of the framework

Unreal's gameplay framework is a set of classes that hand off responsibility cleanly:

ClassOwns / answers
GameModeThe rules: which Pawn, Controller, HUD classes to use; who wins; spawning.
Pawn / CharacterThe physical thing in the world that can be possessed. Character adds a CharacterMovementComponent + capsule.
PlayerControllerThe player's will — it receives input and tells the Pawn what to do. Persists even when the Pawn dies.
PlayerState / GameStateReplicated data: a player's score; the match's shared state.
Mental model.
The Controller is the brain/soul; the Pawn is the body. Possession connects them. The GameMode is the referee that exists only on the server/host.

A Character with movement

Create a Blueprint based on Character (not plain Pawn) — call it BP_PlayerChar. It already has a CapsuleComponent root, a SkeletalMesh, and a CharacterMovementComponent that handles walking, gravity, and jumping for you. Add a SpringArm and a Camera on the end for a third-person view; enable Use Pawn Control Rotation on the spring arm.

Enhanced Input (the modern way)

UE5 uses the Enhanced Input system. Create Input Actions (e.g. IA_Move as Axis2D, IA_Look as Axis2D, IA_Jump as Digital) and an Input Mapping Context that binds keys/stick to them. In the Character's BeginPlay, get the local player subsystem and Add Mapping Context.

In the Event Graph, bind the actions:

  • IA_Move → break the Vector2D → Add Movement Input along the controller's forward (Y) and right (X) vectors.
  • IA_Look → Add Controller Yaw/Pitch Input.
  • IA_Jump → call Jump on Started, Stop Jumping on Completed.

The same wiring in C++

PlayerChar.cpp (excerpt)
void APlayerChar::SetupPlayerInputComponent(UInputComponent* Input) { auto* EI = Cast<UEnhancedInputComponent>(Input); EI->BindAction(MoveAction, ETriggerEvent::Triggered, this, &APlayerChar::Move); EI->BindAction(JumpAction, ETriggerEvent::Started, this, &ACharacter::Jump); } void APlayerChar::Move(const FInputActionValue& Value) { const FVector2D Axis = Value.Get<FVector2D>(); const FRotator Yaw(0, Controller->GetControlRotation().Yaw, 0); AddMovementInput(FRotationMatrix(Yaw).GetUnitAxis(EAxis::X), Axis.Y); AddMovementInput(FRotationMatrix(Yaw).GetUnitAxis(EAxis::Y), Axis.X); }

Wiring the GameMode

Create BP_GameMode from GameModeBase. In its Class Defaults, set Default Pawn Class = BP_PlayerChar and Player Controller Class = your controller (or the default). Then in Project Settings > Maps & Modes, set Default GameMode to BP_GameMode — or override it per-level in World Settings > GameMode Override.

Two reasons you cannot move.
(1) No mapping context was added — check BeginPlay. (2) The GameMode is not using your Character — check Default Pawn Class. A black screen usually means no camera or no PlayerStart.
🎮 Build This: Third-Person Prototype

A possessed Pawn driven through a custom PlayerController and GameMode.

  1. Create BP_PlayerChar (Character) with SpringArm + Camera.
  2. Make IA_Move, IA_Look, IA_Jump and an IMC_Default mapping context; add it on BeginPlay.
  3. Wire movement, look, and jump in the Event Graph.
  4. Create BP_GameMode; set Default Pawn = your character.
  5. Set the GameMode in World Settings, add a PlayerStart, and play.

title: Level Design & Landscape Basics
html:
From blockout to landscape

Indoor spaces are built from meshes; outdoor terrain uses the Landscape system — a heightfield you sculpt and paint. Open the Landscape mode from the mode dropdown in the top-left toolbar (or Shift+2).

Creating a landscape

In Landscape mode > Manage > New, you set:

  • Section Size and Sections Per Component — leave defaults for now; these control LOD granularity.
  • Number of Components — the overall size. A few components (e.g. 8×8) is plenty for a prototype.
  • Material — optionally assign a landscape material now.

Click Create. You get a flat green plane that is one editable Actor.

Sculpting

Switch to the Sculpt sub-tab. Pick a brush and tool:

ToolEffect
SculptRaise terrain (hold Shift to lower)
SmoothSoften jagged areas
FlattenLevel a region to a target height
RampClick two points to build a graded path
Erosion / NoiseAdd natural detail

Adjust Brush Size, Falloff, and Tool Strength in the panel. Paint hills around the edges, carve a valley, and use Flatten to make a buildable plateau.

Use the Ramp tool for paths.
It creates a smooth navigable grade between two heights — far better than freehand sculpting a slope the character can actually climb.

Walkable slopes & navigation

The CharacterMovementComponent has a Walkable Floor Angle (default 44°). Slopes steeper than that block the character. Keep your paths gentle. Check the actual playable angle by walking it in PIE — if the character slides or stops, flatten the grade.

For AI to navigate, add a NavMeshBoundsVolume (from Place Actors), scale it to enclose your landscape, and press P to visualize the green navigable area. If green does not appear on a slope, it is too steep for the agent.

Landscape collision is automatic — but heavy.
Sculpting changes collision in real time. Avoid making one gigantic landscape for a tiny level; the heightfield collision and tessellation cost scales with component count.

Layout principles

  • Sightlines & landmarks: give the player a tall feature to orient by.
  • Readable affordances: a path of flattened ground reads as "go here".
  • Blockout first: place gray-box meshes for structures, validate the flow in PIE, then beautify.
  • Player metrics: build to your character's jump height and step size so traversal feels right.
Test by playing, not looking.
A level only "works" once you walk it. Re-enter PIE after every significant sculpt to confirm the path is traversable and nothing traps the character.
🎮 Build This: Outdoor Playground

A sculpted landscape with hills and a path your third-person character can traverse.

  1. Create a Landscape (~8×8 components) in a new level.
  2. Sculpt hills around the perimeter and a central valley.
  3. Use the Ramp tool to carve a gentle, walkable path up one hill.
  4. Place your BP_GameMode, character, and a PlayerStart on the path.
  5. Play and walk the path; flatten any slope too steep to climb. Add a NavMeshBoundsVolume and press P to check coverage.

title: Materials & Lighting with Lumen
html:
What a material is

A Material in UE5 is a node graph that computes surface properties per pixel. Create one via Content Drawer > + Add > Material, name it M_Floor, and double-click to open the Material Editor. The big node on the right is the Result node with inputs for the PBR model:

InputDrives
Base ColorThe albedo / diffuse color
Metallic0 = dielectric, 1 = metal
Roughness0 = mirror, 1 = matte
NormalSurface bump detail (from a normal map)
Emissive ColorLight the surface emits (glow)

Building a textured material

  1. Drag a Texture Sample node into the graph (hold T and click) and pick a base color texture.
  2. Connect its RGB to Base Color.
  3. Add a Constant (key 1 + click) set to ~0.5 into Roughness.
  4. Plug a normal map's output into Normal.
  5. For tiling, add a TexCoord node, multiply by a scalar, and feed the UVs of your texture samples.
Expose parameters for reuse.
Right-click a Constant and Convert to Parameter, or use a ScalarParameter/VectorParameter. Then create Material Instances from the master material to tweak values without recompiling the shader. This is the core workflow pros use.

Emissive glow for collectibles

For a glowing pickup, plug a bright VectorParameter color into Emissive Color and multiply it by a scalar (e.g. 5–20) to push it beyond 1.0 so it blooms. This emissive surface will also cast light into the scene under Lumen.

Lighting with Lumen

Lumen is UE5's real-time global illumination and reflection system — no light baking required. New projects on the high preset enable it by default. Verify in Project Settings > Rendering: Dynamic Global Illumination Method = Lumen and Reflection Method = Lumen.

Core lights you place:

  • Directional Light — the sun; set its rotation for time of day.
  • Sky Light — captures ambient sky color; set to Real Time Capture so it updates with Lumen.
  • SkyAtmosphere + ExponentialHeightFog — sky color and depth.
  • Point / Spot / Rect Lights — local artificial light.
Lumen needs lit surfaces to bounce.
If GI looks flat or dark, check: the Sky Light is set to Real Time Capture, surfaces are not pure black (no albedo = no bounce), and Generate Mesh Distance Fields is on in Rendering settings (Lumen relies on it for software tracing).
Movable lights, no bake.
Keep lights set to Movable when using Lumen — its strength is fully dynamic lighting. Stationary/Static lighting with lightmaps is the older baked path you generally do not mix with Lumen GI.
🎮 Build This: Lit Collectathon Space

Textured surfaces and emissive glowing collectibles under Lumen.

  1. Author M_Floor with a tiling base color + roughness; apply to your floor and walls.
  2. Make M_Glow with an emissive VectorParameter (×10) and create two Material Instances in different colors.
  3. Apply the glow instances to your pickup meshes; confirm they bloom and cast colored light.
  4. Add a Directional Light, a Sky Light (Real Time Capture), and SkyAtmosphere.
  5. Confirm Lumen GI is on, then dim the sun and watch the emissive pickups light the room.

title: Physics & Gameplay Interaction
html:
Simulating physics

Any Primitive Component (Static Mesh, etc.) can simulate rigid-body physics. Select a Static Mesh Actor, and in Details under Physics enable Simulate Physics. Set Mass (override or via density) and toggle Enable Gravity. The mesh's Collision presets must be set so it actually collides — use PhysicsActor or BlockAllDynamic.

Simulate Physics needs collision geometry.
A mesh with no simple collision will either fall through the world or refuse to simulate. Open the mesh and add box/sphere collision, or it will warn "using complex collision for simulation" — which is expensive and often broken for dynamics.

Applying force and impulse

To shove a physics body, call on its component:

  • AddImpulse — instant velocity change (a hit/explosion). Pass a world-space vector.
  • AddForce — continuous push applied each tick (wind, thrust).
  • AddRadialImpulse — explosion from a point with falloff.

Detecting interaction: three flavors

MechanismUse for
Overlap eventsTriggers, pickups, zones — needs Generate Overlap Events on both
Hit eventsBlocking collisions / physical contact (OnComponentHit)
Line/Sweep TraceInstant raycasts: hitscan weapons, line-of-sight, ground checks

For a pickup, give the collectible a Sphere collision set to OverlapAllDynamic and bind OnComponentBeginOverlap: check the other Actor is the player, add score, then Destroy Actor.

A line-trace weapon in C++

Fire (line trace + impulse)
void AWeapon::Fire() { FVector Start = Camera->GetComponentLocation(); FVector End = Start + Camera->GetForwardVector() * 10000.f; FHitResult Hit; FCollisionQueryParams Params; Params.AddIgnoredActor(GetOwner()); bool bHit = GetWorld()->LineTraceSingleByChannel( Hit, Start, End, ECC_Visibility, Params); if (bHit) { DrawDebugLine(GetWorld(), Start, Hit.ImpactPoint, FColor::Red, false, 1.f); UPrimitiveComponent* Comp = Hit.GetComponent(); if (Comp && Comp->IsSimulatingPhysics()) { FVector Dir = (End - Start).GetSafeNormal(); Comp->AddImpulseAtLocation(Dir * 50000.f, Hit.ImpactPoint); } } }

The Blueprint equivalent: a LineTraceByChannel node from the camera location forward; branch on Return Value; from Out Hit break the struct, get the Hit Component, and call AddImpulseAtLocation.

Trace channels matter.
ECC_Visibility ignores invisible blockers; ECC_Camera is for camera collision. For gameplay you often define a custom Weapon channel in Project Settings > Collision so traces hit exactly what you intend.
Draw your debug lines.
DrawDebugLine (or the Draw Debug option on trace nodes) is the fastest way to see where your trace actually went. Half of "my gun does nothing" bugs are the trace pointing the wrong way.
🎮 Build This: Shooter Core

A line-trace weapon that knocks physics targets around the room.

  1. Place several cube/barrel meshes with Simulate Physics on and proper collision.
  2. On the player, add a Fire input action.
  3. On Fire, line-trace from the camera forward; draw a debug line.
  4. If the hit component is simulating physics, AddImpulseAtLocation along the shot direction.
  5. Play and shoot the props — they should tumble and scatter.

title: UI with UMG
html:
What UMG is

UMG (Unreal Motion Graphics) is the widget-based UI system. You design screens visually in a Widget Blueprint and drive them with the same Blueprint/C++ logic as gameplay. Create one via + Add > User Interface > Widget Blueprint; name it WBP_HUD.

The Designer and the Palette

A Widget Blueprint has two tabs: Designer (drag-and-drop layout) and Graph (logic). From the Palette drag widgets onto the canvas:

WidgetRole
Canvas PanelFree-position container (the root by default)
Horizontal/Vertical BoxAuto-arranging layout containers
Text / ImageDisplay label and graphics
ButtonClickable, fires OnClicked
Progress BarHealth/stamina fill
Anchors prevent broken layouts.
Every widget on a Canvas Panel has Anchors — the point it sticks to as resolution changes. Anchor a health bar to the bottom-left, a score to the top-right. Without anchors, your HUD drifts off-screen on other displays.

Binding to gameplay data

Static text is useless for a HUD. Bind it. Select a Text widget; next to its Text property click Bind > Create Binding. This makes a function that runs every frame and returns the value to display — e.g. cast the player character and return its Health as text.

Property Bind runs every frame.
Per-frame bindings are convenient but can be a performance trap with many widgets. The pro pattern is event-driven updates: when health changes, the character calls an UpdateHealth event on the HUD widget that sets the Progress Bar percent once.

Creating and showing widgets

A widget asset is just a class — it does nothing until you instantiate and add it to the viewport. In your PlayerController BeginPlay:

Show HUD (C++)
void AMyPlayerController::BeginPlay() { Super::BeginPlay(); if (HUDClass) // a TSubclassOf<UUserWidget> set in defaults { HUD = CreateWidget<UUserWidget>(this, HUDClass); HUD->AddToViewport(); } }

In Blueprint that is Create Widget (class = WBP_HUD) → Add to Viewport, ideally storing the reference in a variable so you can update it.

A main menu

Make WBP_MainMenu with a title and two buttons (Play, Quit). On the Play button's OnClicked: call Open Level (by name) with your gameplay map. On Quit: Quit Game. For menus, set the input mode so the mouse works:

  • Call Set Input Mode UI Only on the PlayerController and Set Show Mouse Cursor = true.
  • For gameplay HUD, use Set Input Mode Game Only and hide the cursor.
Put the menu in its own level.
A dedicated MainMenu map with a simple GameMode that creates the menu widget keeps your gameplay map clean and load fast.
🎮 Build This: Shooter Shell

A UMG main menu plus an in-game HUD showing health and a score count.

  1. Build WBP_MainMenu: title, Play (Open Level), Quit buttons; show it from a MainMenu-level GameMode with UI input mode.
  2. Build WBP_HUD: a Progress Bar (anchored bottom-left) and a Score Text (anchored top-right).
  3. Give the player a Health and Score variable.
  4. Add HUD to viewport in the PlayerController BeginPlay (Game input mode).
  5. Update the bar/text via events when health changes or a collectible is grabbed.

title: Capstone: A Shooter/Collectathon & Packaging
html:
Assembling the whole game

Everything so far becomes one project. The capstone is a small third-person shooter or collectathon. Decide the core loop first, then connect the systems you have built:

  • Framework: GameMode sets your Character + PlayerController (Lesson 4).
  • Level: a lit landscape or interior with collision-correct geometry (Lessons 2, 5, 6).
  • Mechanic: line-trace weapon knocking physics targets, OR overlap collectibles that increment score (Lesson 7).
  • UI: main menu + HUD with health/score, plus a win/lose screen (Lesson 8).

A win condition with GameState

Track progress on the GameMode/GameState, not scattered across Actors. For a collectathon: store TotalPickups and Collected. Each pickup, on overlap, calls a GameMode function that increments Collected and checks for the win. On win, open a results level or show a WBP_WinScreen and call Set Game Paused.

Centralize game rules.
When a pickup is destroyed, it should report to the GameMode rather than the HUD reading every pickup each frame. The GameMode then fires one event the HUD listens to. One source of truth, no per-frame scanning.

Pre-package checklist

  1. Set Project Settings > Maps & Modes: Game Default Map = your menu, and confirm the gameplay map opens via the Play button.
  2. Set Project Settings > Description: project name, version, company.
  3. Pick the Build Configuration: Shipping for the final build (no debug overhead), Development while testing.
  4. Remove debug draws and on-screen Print String nodes.

Packaging a build

Use Platforms > Windows > Package Project (toolbar). Choose an output folder. UE cooks all assets for the target platform and produces a runnable build.

terminal — command-line cook (optional)
$ RunUAT.bat BuildCookRun -project="D:/UE/Shooter/Shooter.uproject" \ -platform=Win64 -clientconfig=Shipping -cook -stage -pak -archive \ -archivedirectory="D:/Builds" ... cooking 412 assets ... BUILD SUCCESSFUL
Common packaging failures.
(1) No compiler — packaging C++ projects requires Visual Studio/Xcode installed. (2) Missing maps — if you Open Level by name, that level must be added under List of maps to include in a packaged build. (3) Editor-only assets referenced in gameplay get stripped and crash. Always test the packaged build, not just PIE.
Cook errors are in the log.
If packaging fails, read Saved/Logs and the Output Log — it names the exact asset or shader that broke. Fix that one thing and re-cook; never guess.

The result is a folder with an .exe (Windows) you can zip and share. Test it on a clean machine if possible to catch missing redistributables.

🎮 Build This: The Capstone

A complete small third-person shooter or collectathon, packaged.

  1. Build a lit level (landscape or interior) with your character and GameMode.
  2. Place the core mechanic: trace-weapon targets or overlap collectibles with score on the GameMode.
  3. Add main menu, in-game HUD (health/score), and a win screen driven by a GameState win condition.
  4. Clean up debug output; set Default Maps and Shipping config.
  5. Package for Windows, then run and play the packaged .exe end to end.
